Subject: HASS
ContentDescription: The people in their family, where they were born and raised, and how they are related to each other
Elaboration: Discussing the members of a family (for example, mother, father, caregiver, sister, brother, grandparent, auntie, uncle, cousin) and acknowledging other varieties of kinship structure that may be represented in the cultural backgrounds of students (for example, cousin-brother and mother-auntie in First Nations Australian families)

Subject: HASS
ContentDescription: The people in their family, where they were born and raised, and how they are related to each other
Elaboration: Creating drawings of family members accompanied by information collected from questions and observations to share details of that person’s life, including the places they were born and raised

Subject: HASS
ContentDescription: The people in their family, where they were born and raised, and how they are related to each other
Elaboration: Creating concept maps of their family with pictures or photographs to show connections to those people who make up their family

Subject: HASS
ContentDescription: The celebrations and commemorations of significant events shared with their families and others
Elaboration: Responding to a provided calendar of events that students, their families and friends celebrate or commemorate; for example, birthdays; religious festivals such as Easter, Ramadan, Buddha’s Birthday, Feast of Passover and Coming of the Light; family reunions; cultural festivals; and community commemorations such as NAIDOC Week and Anzac Day, and discussing why they are important

Subject: HASS
ContentDescription: The celebrations and commemorations of significant events shared with their families and others
Elaboration: Discussing ways of celebrating these significant occasions; for example, special meals, family gatherings, visiting special places, and the role of art, music, telling stories and handing on traditions from generation to generation for First Nations Australians

Subject: HASS
ContentDescription: The features of familiar places they belong to, why some places are special and how places can be looked after
Elaboration: Identifying the places, communities, Country/Place they live in and belong to, and why that place may be special to them; for example, a neighbourhood, suburb, town or rural locality, or community

Subject: HASS
ContentDescription: The features of familiar places they belong to, why some places are special and how places can be looked after
Elaboration: Exploring the features of their own place, and places they are familiar with or aware of; for example, places they have visited, places family members have come from, imaginary places in stories, or places featured on television and websites, in books or ebooks

Subject: HASS
ContentDescription: The features of familiar places they belong to, why some places are special and how places can be looked after
Elaboration: Identifying reasons why people live in or visit places, such as the provision of basic needs (water, food, shelter), to enhance lives (holiday places, places for recreation, for religious observance) and to maintain cultural connections to Country/Place

Subject: HASS
ContentDescription: The features of familiar places they belong to, why some places are special and how places can be looked after
Elaboration: Discussing different ways they could or do contribute to caring for special places, including those that are unique; for example, planting trees for a local endangered species, cleaning up litter at a local park or beach, or planting flora in a local wetland

Subject: HASS
ContentDescription: The importance of Country/Place to First Nations Australians and the Country/Place on which the school is located
Elaboration: Identifying how and why the words “Country/Place” are used by First Nations Australians for the places they belong to

Subject: HASS
ContentDescription: The importance of Country/Place to First Nations Australians and the Country/Place on which the school is located
Elaboration: Identifying and using the name of the local First Nations Australian language group or groups

Subject: HASS
ContentDescription: The importance of Country/Place to First Nations Australians and the Country/Place on which the school is located
Elaboration: Listening and responding to invited members of the Traditional Owner group talking about Country/Place, and places of cultural and historical significance to the First Nations Australian community in the local neighbourhood, suburb, town or rural area

Subject: HASS
ContentDescription: The importance of Country/Place to First Nations Australians and the Country/Place on which the school is located
Elaboration: Identifying local places of significance for First Nations Australians in the local area

Subject: HASS
ContentDescription: Pose questions about familiar objects, people, places and events
Elaboration: Posing questions about family and places, such as “How old was I?”, “Where was I?” and “What was I doing?”, having explored sources relating to their own life; for example, family interviews, photographs, stories, film, classmates’ paintings, excursions to places

Subject: HASS
ContentDescription: Pose questions about familiar objects, people, places and events
Elaboration: Posing questions about the features of places and how students can look after them, after being encouraged to observe them using various senses

Subject: HASS
ContentDescription: Pose questions about familiar objects, people, places and events
Elaboration: Posing questions about what makes events and places special; for example “What special events do my family celebrate?”, “What makes my favourite places special?”

Subject: HASS
ContentDescription: Sort and record information including pictorial timelines and locations on pictorial maps or models
Elaboration: Sorting and displaying sources (for example, historical sources such as pictures, photographs and family mementoes) to organise a display about a family member or significant family event and creating pictorial timelines, such as adding photos or drawings of significant events as they happen, to create a timeline of events over the year

Subject: HASS
ContentDescription: Sort and record information including pictorial timelines and locations on pictorial maps or models
Elaboration: Identifying features on a provided pictorial map or oblique aerial photograph of a familiar place and linking the representation of specific features to pictures they have drawn of those features; for example, using a pictorial map of a visited site such as a public garden or an oblique aerial photograph of their school to find familiar features, and then linking drawings of those features with lines to the features in the map or aerial photograph

Subject: HASS
ContentDescription: Share a perspective on information, such as stories about significant events and special places
Elaboration: Sharing aspects of events special to past generations of their family from provided stories and discussing why those events and places are special

Subject: HASS
ContentDescription: Share a perspective on information, such as stories about significant events and special places
Elaboration: Identifying a place in their local area that they like, and talking about why they like it and how they could care for it

Subject: HASS
ContentDescription: Draw conclusions in response to questions
Elaboration: Identifying the main people in their family and explaining how they are related

Subject: HASS
ContentDescription: Draw conclusions in response to questions
Elaboration: Identifying the most important celebrations and commemorations in their lives and those of their family

Subject: HASS
ContentDescription: Draw conclusions in response to questions
Elaboration: Exploring the location and features of places they belong to and what makes those places special

Subject: HASS
ContentDescription: Draw conclusions in response to questions
Elaboration: Suggesting ways that they are going to care for their classroom, bedroom or playground

Subject: HASS
ContentDescription: Share narratives and observations, using sources and terms about the past and places
Elaboration: Creating a display about a special place or family event using photographs, artefacts and/or drawings

Subject: HASS
ContentDescription: Share narratives and observations, using sources and terms about the past and places
Elaboration: Using terms about time when talking about their experiences; for example, “then”, “now”, “yesterday”, “today”, “tomorrow”

Subject: HASS
ContentDescription: Share narratives and observations, using sources and terms about the past and places
Elaboration: Using appropriate terms to describe the direction and location of a place such as “near and far”, “above and below”, “beside and opposite”
